quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary function of the digestive system?

options.text: To break down food into nutrients that the body can absorb and use for energy, growth, and repair
To transport oxygen to all cells in the body
To filter waste products from the blood
To coordinate body movements and reflexes

quiz_questions.explanation: The digestive system breaks down food through mechanical and chemical processes into small molecules (amino acids, simple sugars, fatty acids) that can be absorbed into the bloodstream and used by cells for energy, building materials, and metabolic functions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where does digestion begin?

options.text: The mouth — through chewing (mechanical) and salivary enzymes (chemical)
The stomach — where acid first contacts food
The small intestine — where most absorption occurs
The esophagus — where peristalsis starts

quiz_questions.explanation: Digestion begins in the mouth. Teeth mechanically break food into smaller pieces (mastication), while salivary amylase begins chemically breaking down starches into sugars. The tongue also helps mix food with saliva and forms it into a bolus for swallowing.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the esophagus?

options.text: A muscular tube that moves food from the throat to the stomach by peristalsis
The organ that produces stomach acid
The first section of the small intestine
A gland that secretes digestive enzymes

quiz_questions.explanation: The esophagus is a muscular tube about 25 cm long that connects the pharynx (throat) to the stomach. It uses peristalsis — rhythmic, wave-like muscle contractions — to push food downward. This works regardless of body position, which is why you can swallow while lying down.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the main role of the stomach in digestion?

options.text: To churn food and use hydrochloric acid and pepsin to chemically break down proteins
To absorb most nutrients into the bloodstream
To produce bile for fat digestion
To reabsorb water from digested food

quiz_questions.explanation: The stomach is a muscular organ that mechanically churns food and chemically digests it. Parietal cells secrete hydrochloric acid (HCl) creating a highly acidic environment (pH 1.5–3.5), and chief cells release pepsinogen, which acid activates into pepsin — a protease that breaks down proteins.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where does most nutrient absorption occur in the digestive system?

options.text: The small intestine
The stomach
The large intestine
The esophagus

quiz_questions.explanation: The small intestine is where approximately 90% of nutrient absorption takes place. Its inner wall is lined with villi and microvilli, creating an enormous surface area (~250 m²) for absorbing amino acids, sugars, fatty acids, vitamins, and minerals into the bloodstream.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the large intestine primarily absorb?

options.text: Water and some electrolytes
Proteins and amino acids
Fats and fat-soluble vitamins
Simple sugars like glucose

quiz_questions.explanation: The large intestine (colon) primarily absorbs water and electrolytes (sodium, potassium, chloride) from the remaining digestive material. It reclaims about 1.5 liters of water per day, turning liquid chyme into solid feces. Most nutrients have already been absorbed in the small intestine.

quiz_questions.question_text: What organ produces bile?

options.text: The liver
The gallbladder
The pancreas
The stomach

quiz_questions.explanation: The liver produces bile continuously. Bile is then stored and concentrated in the gallbladder until needed. When fatty food enters the duodenum, bile is released to emulsify fats — breaking large fat globules into smaller droplets so lipase can digest them more efficiently.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is peristalsis?

options.text: Rhythmic, wave-like muscle contractions that push food through the digestive tract
The chemical breakdown of food by enzymes
The absorption of nutrients through intestinal walls
The mixing of food with stomach acid

quiz_questions.explanation: Peristalsis is a coordinated wave of smooth muscle contraction behind a food mass (bolus), combined with relaxation ahead of it, that propels food through the entire GI tract. It works regardless of gravity — even if you ate while upside-down, food would still reach the stomach.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between mechanical and chemical digestion?

options.text: Mechanical digestion physically breaks food into smaller pieces; chemical digestion uses enzymes to break molecular bonds
Mechanical digestion occurs only in the stomach; chemical digestion occurs only in the intestines
Mechanical digestion uses enzymes; chemical digestion uses physical force
There is no difference — both terms mean the same thing

quiz_questions.explanation: Mechanical digestion includes chewing, churning in the stomach, and segmentation in the intestines — it increases surface area but doesn't change chemical composition. Chemical digestion uses enzymes (amylase, pepsin, lipase, trypsin) to break covalent bonds in macromolecules, converting them into absorbable monomers. Both work together throughout the GI tract.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the correct order of organs food passes through in the digestive tract?

options.text: Mouth → pharynx → esophagus → stomach → small intestine → large intestine → rectum → anus
Mouth → esophagus → pharynx → stomach → large intestine → small intestine → anus
Mouth → stomach → esophagus → small intestine → large intestine → rectum
Pharynx → mouth → stomach → small intestine → large intestine → anus

quiz_questions.explanation: The GI tract is a continuous tube about 9 meters long. Food travels: mouth (chewing, amylase) → pharynx (throat) → esophagus (peristalsis) → stomach (acid, pepsin) → small intestine (digestion/absorption) → large intestine (water absorption) → rectum → anus. Accessory organs (liver, gallbladder, pancreas) add secretions but food does not pass through them.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'chyme'?

options.text: The thick, acidic, semi-liquid mixture of partially digested food that leaves the stomach and enters the small intestine
The ball of chewed food formed in the mouth
The final solid waste product eliminated from the body
The bile-fat mixture in the small intestine

quiz_questions.explanation: Chyme is the soupy, acidic mixture formed after the stomach churns food with hydrochloric acid and pepsin for 2–6 hours. It is released in small squirts through the pyloric sphincter into the duodenum. The term 'bolus' refers to the food ball in the mouth/esophagus; chyme is the next stage.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'villi'?

options.text: Tiny finger-like projections lining the small intestine that increase surface area for nutrient absorption
Cells that produce stomach acid
Enzymes that break down fats
Folds in the stomach wall that allow it to stretch

quiz_questions.explanation: Villi are ~1 mm tall finger-like projections that cover the inner wall of the small intestine. Each villus contains a capillary network (for absorbing amino acids, sugars, and water-soluble vitamins) and a lacteal (for absorbing fats). The cells on each villus also have microvilli (brush border), further increasing the total absorptive surface area to about 250 m².

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'pepsin'?

options.text: A protease enzyme in the stomach that breaks down proteins; it is activated from pepsinogen by hydrochloric acid
A hormone that stimulates appetite
A type of bile salt that emulsifies fats
An enzyme in saliva that digests starch

quiz_questions.explanation: Pepsin is the primary protein-digesting enzyme of the stomach. Chief cells secrete it in an inactive form (pepsinogen) to prevent self-digestion. Hydrochloric acid from parietal cells activates pepsinogen into pepsin, which works optimally at pH 1.5–2.5 to cleave peptide bonds in proteins.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'emulsification' mean in the context of digestion?

options.text: The process where bile salts break large fat globules into smaller droplets, increasing the surface area for lipase to act on
The chemical breakdown of proteins into amino acids
The absorption of water in the large intestine
The conversion of starch into glucose

quiz_questions.explanation: Emulsification is not chemical digestion — bile salts do not break chemical bonds. Instead, they act as biological detergents, breaking large fat globules into tiny droplets (an emulsion). This dramatically increases the surface area available for pancreatic lipase to chemically digest fats into fatty acids and monoglycerides.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'sphincter' in the digestive system?

options.text: A ring of smooth muscle that opens and closes to regulate the passage of material between digestive organs
A type of digestive enzyme
A fold in the stomach lining
A cell that absorbs nutrients in the intestines

quiz_questions.explanation: Sphincters are circular muscles that act as valves. Key digestive sphincters include: the lower esophageal (cardiac) sphincter (prevents acid reflux from stomach to esophagus), the pyloric sphincter (controls release of chyme from stomach to duodenum), the ileocecal valve (small to large intestine), and the anal sphincters (control defecation).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'salivary amylase'?

options.text: An enzyme in saliva that begins the chemical digestion of starch into maltose (a sugar)
A type of mucus that lubricates food in the mouth
A stomach enzyme that digests proteins
A bile salt that emulsifies fats

quiz_questions.explanation: Salivary amylase (also called ptyalin) is secreted by the salivary glands and begins breaking down starch into maltose right in the mouth. This is why crackers taste sweet after prolonged chewing. Salivary amylase works at a neutral pH (~6.8) and is inactivated once it reaches the acidic stomach.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'rugae'?

options.text: Large folds in the stomach lining that allow the stomach to stretch and expand when filled with food
Finger-like projections in the small intestine for absorption
Cells that produce hydrochloric acid
Muscles that create peristaltic waves

quiz_questions.explanation: Rugae are large, accordion-like folds in the inner wall of the stomach. When the stomach is empty, rugae are prominent; as it fills with food (up to ~1.5 liters), these folds flatten out, allowing the stomach to expand without increasing internal pressure dramatically.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'bolus'?

options.text: A soft, rounded mass of chewed food mixed with saliva that is ready to be swallowed
The liquid mixture of digested food in the stomach
A type of enzyme produced by the pancreas
A section of the large intestine

quiz_questions.explanation: After food is chewed (mastication) and mixed with saliva in the mouth, the tongue shapes it into a bolus — a compact, lubricated mass that can be easily swallowed. The bolus travels down the esophagus via peristalsis. Once in the stomach, the bolus is further processed into chyme.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'lacteal'?

options.text: A lymphatic capillary inside each intestinal villus that absorbs dietary fats packaged as chylomicrons
An enzyme that digests lactose in milk
A type of cell in the stomach that produces acid
A section of the large intestine

quiz_questions.explanation: Each villus in the small intestine contains a lacteal — a specialized lymphatic capillary. Absorbed fats are packaged into large chylomicrons that are too big to enter blood capillaries but can enter the wider lacteals. From there, fats travel through the lymphatic system and eventually enter the bloodstream via the thoracic duct.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'gut microbiome'?

options.text: The community of trillions of bacteria and other microorganisms living in the digestive tract, especially the large intestine
The layer of mucus lining the stomach wall
The enzymes secreted by the pancreas
The network of blood vessels in the intestinal wall

quiz_questions.explanation: The gut microbiome consists of ~100 trillion microorganisms (mostly bacteria) residing primarily in the large intestine. They perform essential functions: synthesizing vitamins (K, B12, biotin), fermenting dietary fiber into short-chain fatty acids, training the immune system, and competing with pathogenic bacteria for resources (colonization resistance).

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient eats a plain cracker and notices it starts to taste sweet after chewing for about 30 seconds without swallowing. A dental student observing asks: 'How is starch turning sweet in the mouth when there's no heat or acid?' The answer involves:

options.text: Salivary amylase cleaves starch (a polysaccharide) into maltose (a disaccharide sugar) right in the mouth — this enzymatic chemical digestion begins before food even reaches the stomach
The teeth grind the cracker so finely that its natural sugars are released
Stomach acid travels up the esophagus to digest the cracker in the mouth
Taste buds change over time, making everything taste sweeter

quiz_questions.explanation: Salivary amylase (ptyalin) is secreted by salivary glands (parotid, submandibular, sublingual) at a rate of ~1.5 liters/day. It hydrolyzes α-1,4-glycosidic bonds in starch, producing maltose and dextrin — both taste sweet. This works at the mouth's neutral pH (~6.8). The enzyme is inactivated once it reaches the stomach's acid environment. This demonstrates that chemical digestion doesn't require extreme conditions — just the right enzyme. Pancreatic amylase later completes starch digestion in the duodenum.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronaut in the International Space Station eats a meal in zero gravity. Despite the absence of gravity, food still moves smoothly from the esophagus to the stomach. A mission controller asks: 'How is food reaching the stomach without gravity pulling it down?'

options.text: Peristalsis — coordinated waves of smooth muscle contraction behind the bolus and relaxation ahead of it propel food independently of gravity
The astronaut must drink water to wash food down in zero gravity
Air pressure inside the space station pushes food into the stomach
The esophagus uses suction from the stomach to pull food downward

quiz_questions.explanation: Peristalsis is a muscular mechanism, not a gravitational one. Circular smooth muscle behind the food bolus contracts while the muscle ahead relaxes, creating a directional wave that pushes food along at about 2–4 cm/sec. Longitudinal muscles shorten the tract ahead of the bolus. This is why you can swallow while upside down and why astronauts eat normally in microgravity. Peristalsis occurs throughout the entire GI tract — esophagus, stomach, intestines.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pathologist examines a stomach biopsy and notes three distinct cell types: parietal cells, chief cells, and mucous cells. A medical student asks: 'The stomach produces acid strong enough to dissolve metal — yet the stomach doesn't digest itself. How?' The pathologist explains the triple-defense system:

options.text: Mucous cells produce a thick alkaline mucus barrier (~1 mm), bicarbonate trapped in the mucus neutralizes acid at the surface (pH gradient from ~2 to ~7), and rapid epithelial cell turnover replaces damaged cells every 3–6 days
The stomach wall is made of acid-resistant bone-like tissue
Pepsin only digests food proteins and cannot recognize human tissue
The acid is only released when food is present and immediately neutralized after

quiz_questions.explanation: The stomach's self-protection involves three mechanisms: (1) thick mucus layer secreted by surface mucous cells forms a physical barrier; (2) bicarbonate ions (HCO₃⁻) secreted into the mucus create a pH gradient — pH ~1-2 in the lumen but ~7 at the epithelial surface; (3) the gastric epithelium replaces itself every 3-6 days, the fastest turnover rate of any tissue. When NSAIDs (like aspirin/ibuprofen) inhibit prostaglandin production, mucus and bicarbonate secretion decrease — explaining why chronic NSAID use causes gastric ulcers.

quiz_questions.question_text: After a fatty steak dinner, a patient's gallbladder contracts and releases bile into the duodenum. A student asks: 'Bile isn't an enzyme — so how does it help digest fat?' The gastroenterologist explains the physics:

options.text: Bile salts are amphipathic (hydrophobic and hydrophilic ends) — they break large fat globules into tiny droplets (emulsification), increasing the surface area by 1000× so pancreatic lipase can access and digest the fat efficiently
Bile chemically breaks the bonds in triglycerides to release fatty acids
Bile dissolves fats completely, turning them into water-soluble molecules
Bile kills bacteria that would otherwise consume dietary fat

quiz_questions.explanation: Bile salts are biological detergents with a hydrophobic side (attracts fat) and a hydrophilic side (attracts water). When mixed with fats, they break large globules into microscopic droplets — an emulsion. This increases surface area dramatically, allowing water-soluble pancreatic lipase to access the fat-water interface and hydrolyze triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides. The hormone CCK (cholecystokinin) triggers gallbladder contraction when fat enters the duodenum. Bile also helps absorb fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient is diagnosed with both Type 1 diabetes (insulin deficiency) and exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (poor fat digestion). The endocrinologist explains that both conditions involve the same organ. How can one organ cause two completely different diseases?

options.text: The pancreas has dual functions: exocrine acinar cells (~99%) produce digestive enzymes/bicarbonate, while endocrine islets of Langerhans (~1%) produce insulin and glucagon — damage to different regions causes different diseases
The pancreas only has one function, and the two conditions are unrelated
Diabetes is caused by the liver, not the pancreas
The pancreas only produces insulin; digestive enzymes come from the stomach

quiz_questions.explanation: The pancreas is unique as both an exocrine and endocrine gland. Exocrine (~99%): acinar cells secrete digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, trypsin, chymotrypsin) and bicarbonate into the duodenum via the pancreatic duct. Endocrine (~1%): islets of Langerhans contain beta cells (insulin — lowers blood glucose) and alpha cells (glucagon — raises blood glucose). Pancreatitis damages exocrine tissue, causing malabsorption and steatorrhea (fatty stools). Autoimmune destruction of beta cells causes Type 1 diabetes. Both can coexist.

quiz_questions.question_text: A celiac disease patient accidentally eats a wheat pasta dish. Within hours, they experience severe bloating, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. A biopsy taken months later shows flattened villi in the jejunum. An immunologist explains the cascade of damage:

options.text: Gluten-derived gliadin peptides trigger an autoimmune response — T-cells attack the intestinal epithelium, destroying villi (villous atrophy) and drastically reducing the surface area for nutrient absorption
Wheat physically scratches the intestinal walls, wearing down the villi over time
Gluten blocks the pyloric sphincter, causing food to back up into the stomach
The pancreas shuts down enzyme production when gluten is detected

quiz_questions.explanation: Celiac disease involves a complex immune cascade: gliadin peptides from gluten are deamidated by tissue transglutaminase (tTG), then presented to T-cells by HLA-DQ2/DQ8 molecules on antigen-presenting cells. Activated T-cells attack the intestinal epithelium, causing villous atrophy. With flattened villi, the absorptive surface area plummets, leading to malabsorption of iron (anemia), calcium (osteoporosis), fat-soluble vitamins, and other nutrients. A strict lifelong gluten-free diet allows villi to regenerate over months.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient tests positive for Helicobacter pylori and has a bleeding gastric ulcer. The student asks: 'How can a bacterium survive in pH 1.5 stomach acid when most organisms die instantly?' The microbiologist explains:

options.text: H. pylori produces urease enzyme, converting urea to ammonia (NH₃) — ammonia neutralizes surrounding acid, creating a protective alkaline microenvironment; the bacteria also burrows into the mucus layer where pH is closer to neutral
H. pylori has an acid-proof calcium shell that protects it from all pH levels
The bacterium only colonizes the stomach when acid production temporarily stops
H. pylori neutralizes ALL stomach acid, making the entire stomach pH 7

quiz_questions.explanation: H. pylori's survival strategy has three components: (1) urease converts urea → ammonia + CO₂, creating a local alkaline bubble; (2) its spiral shape and flagella allow it to swim through viscous gastric mucus to reach the near-neutral pH zone at the epithelial surface; (3) adhesins anchor it to gastric epithelial cells. Once established, H. pylori causes chronic inflammation that weakens the mucosal barrier, leading to peptic ulcers and increasing stomach cancer risk. Triple therapy (2 antibiotics + proton pump inhibitor) eradicates it in ~85% of cases.

quiz_questions.question_text: After a meal rich in fat, a patient's lacteals absorb chylomicrons that eventually enter the bloodstream. A nursing student is confused: 'Why do fats enter the lymphatic system instead of going directly into the blood like sugars and amino acids?'

options.text: Chylomicrons (75–1200 nm) are too large to fit through blood capillary pores (~5–10 nm) — lymphatic lacteals have wider, more permeable openings that accommodate these large lipoprotein particles
Fats are toxic to red blood cells and must be detoxified in lymph nodes first
The lymphatic system converts fats to glucose before releasing them into blood
Blood capillaries reject all lipid molecules without exception

quiz_questions.explanation: After lipase digests triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides, intestinal epithelial cells reassemble them into triglycerides and package them with cholesterol and proteins into chylomicrons — large lipoprotein particles. Blood capillaries have tight junctions with small fenestrations (~5-10 nm), making them impermeable to chylomicrons. Lacteals have larger gaps between endothelial cells that allow chylomicrons to enter. They travel through lymphatic vessels and enter blood at the thoracic duct-subclavian vein junction. Short- and medium-chain fatty acids are small enough to enter blood capillaries directly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surgeon performs a cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal) for gallstones. The patient's worried spouse asks: 'How will she digest fat without a gallbladder?' The surgeon reassures them. What is the explanation?

options.text: The liver — not the gallbladder — produces bile; after removal, bile simply drips continuously into the duodenum instead of being stored and released in concentrated bursts
The stomach takes over bile production after gallbladder removal
Fat digestion is impossible without a gallbladder — the patient must avoid all fats permanently
The pancreas produces bile as a backup

quiz_questions.explanation: The gallbladder stores and concentrates bile but does NOT produce it. After cholecystectomy, hepatocytes in the liver continue producing bile (~500–1000 mL/day), which flows directly through the common bile duct into the duodenum. Without the gallbladder's storage function, bile trickles continuously rather than being released in concentrated bursts. Patients may experience difficulty with large, high-fat meals (because a large bolus of concentrated bile is unavailable), but can typically manage with smaller, more frequent meals.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person with lactose intolerance drinks a large glass of milk and within two hours experiences severe bloating, gas, and explosive diarrhea. A friend who is lactose tolerant drinks the same milk with no symptoms. A dietitian explains the biochemical difference between them:

options.text: The intolerant person lacks sufficient lactase enzyme — undigested lactose passes to the colon where bacteria ferment it (producing H₂, CO₂, CH₄ gas) and its osmotic effect draws water into the lumen (causing diarrhea); the tolerant person has a genetic mutation maintaining lactase production into adulthood
The intolerant person is allergic to milk proteins (casein), triggering an immune response
Lactose intolerance means the stomach cannot produce enough acid to curdle milk
The intolerant person's bile cannot emulsify milk fat

quiz_questions.explanation: Lactase is a brush-border enzyme in the small intestine that hydrolyzes lactose into glucose + galactose. Most humans naturally downregulate lactase production after weaning (primary lactose malabsorption — the global norm). Lactase persistence is a genetic adaptation (e.g., C/T-13910 variant) common in populations with a dairy farming history. Without lactase, intact lactose reaches the colon where bacteria metabolize it via fermentation, producing gases (bloating, flatulence) and organic acids. The unabsorbed lactose also creates an osmotic gradient, pulling water into the bowel lumen (diarrhea). Lactase supplements taken with dairy can prevent symptoms.
